fields.Title: Some Bitcoin Mines can be AI Data Centers; Most Can't
fields.Key Takeaways:
• Converting bitcoin mines to AI data centers is not as straightforward as commonly believed
• Mining requires cheap, interruptible, geo-flexible power in remote locations
• AI data centers have different power needs and infrastructure requirements

Detailed analysis arguing that the crypto-miner-to-AI-data-center conversion narrative is overstated. Mining facilities have fundamentally different characteristics than what AI workloads require.

fields.Title: The Growing Compute Shortage
fields.Key Takeaways:
• On-demand GPU capacity is effectively sold out, even older-generation chips seeing high rental rates
• Constraints emerging across entire AI supply chain: GPUs scarce, memory prices surging, TSMC capacity sold out
• Investment implications for AI infrastructure and compute markets

Apollo Global Management analysis of the growing compute shortage. Examines supply constraints across GPUs, memory, and foundry capacity, and their investment implications.

fields.Title: Overview of the Energy Economy (with Datacenters)
fields.Key Takeaways:
• Overview of how data-center demand is reshaping power prices
• Examines intersection of energy economy and AI infrastructure
• Likely covers implications for compute market economics

Newsletter post providing an overview of how data-center demand is reshaping power prices in the energy economy. Connects energy market dynamics to AI infrastructure growth.

fields.Title: Post by Brett Harrison on Compute as Asset Class
fields.Key Takeaways:
• Architect's thesis: compute will mature as a US exchange-traded asset class very quickly
• Fulfilling US government mandate to "accelerate the maturation of a healthy financial market for compute"
• Competition with incumbent players in compute derivatives space

X/Twitter post by Brett Harrison (associated with Architect) discussing the maturation of compute as a US exchange-traded asset class and fulfilling government mandates for healthy compute financial markets.

fields.Title: Post by @ConorMoore on Compute Market Segments
fields.Key Takeaways:
• Identifies the "Compute Middle Market" as lower risk, higher returns
• Estimates $2T in compute spend through 2030
• Segments compute market: Hyperscale ($2bn+), Middle Market, and other tiers

X/Twitter thread by Conor Moore analyzing the compute market structure, identifying a "middle market" segment with attractive risk-return characteristics and projecting massive capital expenditure through 2030.

fields.Title: Building a Robust GPU Index
fields.Key Takeaways:
• Methodology for creating reliable GPU pricing indices
• Approaches to ensure robustness in GPU market data
• Framework for tracking and benchmarking GPU prices

Blog post from Silicon Data discussing the methodology and approach to building reliable GPU pricing indices, likely covering data collection, validation, and benchmarking practices.

fields.Title: SiliconMark vs. InferenceX: Comparing AI Inference Benchmarking Frameworks
fields.Key Takeaways:
• Compares two AI inference benchmarking frameworks for smarter GPU procurement in 2026
• SiliconMark focuses on hardware-centric validation and supports both inference and training benchmarks
• InferenceX (formerly InferenceMax) focuses on LLM-specific inference optimization but is not publicly accessible

Detailed technical comparison of two benchmarking frameworks. SiliconMark benchmarks your own cluster with public access, while InferenceX benchmarks only their own cluster. SiliconMark supports both inference and training workloads, while InferenceX is limited to inference only.

fields.Title: Not All H100s Are Created Equal
fields.Key Takeaways:
• Research from Silicon Data shows up to 38% performance variation within identical H100 GPUs
• Individual chip identity is the dominant factor (32-73% of variance)
• Lenders should require performance certificates before extending credit against GPU pools

Analysis of how identical H100 GPUs show significant performance variation (up to 38%) due to manufacturing differences and cloud provider configurations. Examines implications for GPU-backed lending and risk assessment.

fields.Title: Did You Win the GPU Cloud Lottery? Benchmarking from TFLOPS to Tokens/$
fields.Key Takeaways:
1. Measured 3,500+ physical GPUs across 11 cloud providers, finding up to 38% performance variation for the same GPU model
2. Silicon lottery effects (manufacturing variability) dominate performance variation, not software factors
3. Cloud providers amplify these effects through persistent, systematic second-order effects in their configurations

Large-scale measurement study of 3,500+ physical GPUs across 11 cloud providers. Found up to 38% performance variation for the same GPU model, with silicon lottery effects (manufacturing variability) dominating performance variation rather than software factors.
